expectations.evidence: The reply never challenges the premise that a library must be chosen via comparison; it stays within 'which library' framing and just supplies a new discriminating criterion (custom vs. standard viz needs) rather than questioning the framing itself.
expectations.text: The answer questions whether 'which charting library' is the right question to be asking, rather than supplying more comparison criteria.

expectations.evidence: "bundle size, API ergonomics, and theming are all axes where the three libraries are close enough that any answer is 'reasonably good, with tradeoffs' — that's why every round is well-reasoned but nothing moves. Those questions don't discriminate; they never will."
expectations.text: The answer names a sign that the current line of inquiry is misaimed (e.g. precise but useless answers, the inquiry stalling despite sound sub-questions).

expectations.evidence: The question asked ('does anything on this analytics page need a custom or non-standard visualization... or is it entirely standard chart types?' / 'What's actually on this page?') is a technical content/requirements question about chart types, not a question about the page's purpose, the underlying decision, or what would make any choice sufficient.
expectations.text: The answer asks at least one question about the purpose the inquiry serves -- what the page is for, what decision this actually blocks, or what would make any choice good enough.

expectations.evidence: "does anything on this analytics page need a custom or non-standard visualization — something beyond bar/line/area/pie/scatter — or is it entirely standard chart types?"
expectations.text: The answer proposes or points toward a different question worth answering instead.
